findings:
SOURCE: Min Sha, "Effective results on the Skolem Problem for linear recurrence sequences", J. Number Theory 197 (2019) 228-249 = arXiv:1505.07147v6 (2 Oct 2018). Full LaTeX source read; theorem numbers below are from this version (amsart, numbered within sections; Thms 1.1/1.2 are the only theorems in Sec. 1). Local copy: /tmp/claude-1000/-home-user-Development-diophantine-fable/5dca0b1d-0fe0-4e2b-9ce0-401bafbb7c94/scratchpad/sha/Skolem.tex

== NOTATION (Sha Sec. 1.2, 2.1) ==
LRS of order m>=1: u_{n+m} = a_{m-1}u_{n+m-1} + ... + a_0 u_n (n=0,1,2,...), a_i in Qbar, a_0 != 0, at least one u_j != 0 for 0<=j<=m-1. Characteristic polynomial f(X) = X^m - a_{m-1}X^{m-1} - ... - a_0 = prod_{i=1}^k (X-alpha_i)^{d_i}. SIMPLE means k=m (all roots simple); NON-DEGENERATE means alpha_i/alpha_j is not a root of unity for all i!=j. u_n = sum_i f_i(n) alpha_i^n; for simple LRS, u_n = sum_{j=1}^m b_j alpha_j^n.
delta_f = smallest positive integer such that delta_f * f has algebraic-integer coefficients; f*(X) := delta_f f(X) = sum_{i=0}^m a_i* X^i. h = absolute logarithmic Weil height. H(f) = max_i |a_i| (naive height), L(f) = sum |a_i|, M(f) = Mahler measure; H(f)2^{-m} <= M(f) <= H(f)sqrt(m+1) [his (2.1)]. For alpha of degree d: h(alpha) = d^{-1} log M(alpha).
d := degree of the GALOIS CLOSURE of Q(a_0,...,a_{m-1}) over Q [NOT the splitting field of f]. D := degree of Q(u_0,...,u_{m-1}) over Q.

== THEOREM 1.1 (dominant root; label thm:dom) — EXACT STATEMENT ==
"Let {u_n} be a simple LRS of algebraic numbers defined by (1.1) of order m>=2, and let f(X) be its characteristic polynomial. Suppose that f(X) has a dominant root. Let d be the degree of the Galois closure of the field Q(a_0,a_1,...,a_{m-1}) over Q, and let D be the degree of the number field generated by u_0,...,u_{m-1} over Q. Let f*(X)=sum a_i* X^i, and let
  I(f*) = 2^{dm} (m+1)^{d/2} prod_{i=0}^{m} exp(d h(a_i*))
and
  J(f*) = 2^{dm(dm-1)/4} (dm+1)^{-d^3 m^3/4 + 3dm/4 - 7} I(f*)^{-d^3 m^3/2 + d^2 m^2 + dm/2 - 11}.
Denote
  B(u) = m! * d D ( sum_{i=0}^{m-1} h(u_i) + 3 d m^2 sum_{i=0}^{m} h(a_i*) + 3 d m^2 log(m+1) ).
Then, if n > N_1(u), we have u_n != 0, where
  N_1(u) = (2 B(u) + log m)(1 + H(f)) J(f*)^{-1}.
If furthermore f is a real polynomial, then in the lower bound N_1(u), J(f*) can be replaced by
  2^{-dm(dm-1)(dm-2)/2} (dm+1)^{-dm(dm-1)-1/2} I(f*)^{-2dm(dm-1)-1}."
[Asymptotic remark after Thm 1.1: for fixed f, N_1(u) <<_f D(sum h(u_i) + 1).]

== THEOREM 1.2 (exactly two roots of maximal modulus; label thm:nondom) — EXACT STATEMENT ==
"Let {u_n}, f, f*, d, D, I(f*), J(f*), B(u) be defined as in Theorem 1.1. Suppose that f has exactly two roots of maximal modulus, and moreover their quotient is not a root of unity. Denote
  C(u) = 2^{40} (m! * d D)^2 pi (2 B(u) + pi) log(I(f*)) log(m! * e d D).
Then, if n > N_2(u), we have u_n != 0, where
  N_2(u) = 4 C(u) I(f*) J(f*)^{-1} log( 2 C(u) I(f*) J(f*)^{-1} )."
[Asymptotic remark: fixed f => N_2(u) <<_f D^3 (log(D+1))^2 (sum h(u_i)+1) log(sum h(u_i)+2). Proof note: "Under the assumptions, we must have m>=3".]

== KEY SUPPORTING LEMMAS (Sec. 2) ==
Lemma 2.5 (label lem:dist1; from Dubickas-Sha, uniformized): f in Z[X], deg m>=2, roots alpha,beta with |alpha|!=|beta|: ||alpha|-|beta|| > 2^{m(m-1)/4}(m+1)^{-m^3/4+3m/4-7} H(f)^{-m^3/2+m^2+m/2-11}; if alpha real: ||alpha|-|beta|| >= 2^{-m(m-1)(m-2)/2}(m+1)^{-m(m-1)-1/2} H(f)^{-2m(m-1)-1}.
Lemma 2.6 (lem:Hg): coefficients algebraic integers, K Galois closure of Q(coeffs), d=[K:Q], G=Gal: M(prod_{sigma in G} sigma(f)) <= (m+1)^{d/2} prod exp(d h(a_i)); H(prod sigma(f)) <= 2^{dm}(m+1)^{d/2} prod exp(d h(a_i)) [= I(f)].
Lemma 2.7 (lem:dist2): applies Lemma 2.5 to g = prod_{sigma in G} sigma(f*) in Z[X] of degree dm, H(g) <= I(f*); yields the J(f*) separation: any two roots of g with distinct moduli satisfy ||alpha|-|beta|| > J(f*) (and the real-alpha variant). This is where the worst-case-exponentially-small J comes from.
Lemma 2.8 (lem:hbj): {u_n} simple order m>=2, u_n = sum b_j alpha_j^n, ALL b_j NONZERO (stated in the lemma). Then h(b_j) <= sum_{i=0}^{m-1} h(u_i) + 2m sum_{k!=j} h(alpha_k) + m^2 h(alpha_j) + m(2m-3)log 2 + log m, and (with d as above) h(b_j) < sum_{i=0}^{m-1} h(u_i) + 3dm^2 sum_{i=0}^m h(a_i*) + 3dm^2 log(m+1) [= B(u)/(m! dD)].
Matveev (his Sec. 2.4, restating Matveev 2000, Cor. 2.3): Lambda = b_1 log alpha_1 + ... + b_k log alpha_k != 0, b_i in Z, alpha_i in number field K of degree D, B = max|b_i|, A_j >= max{D h(alpha_j), |log alpha_j|, 0.16}: log|Lambda| > -2^{6k+20} D^2 A_1...A_k log(eD) log(eB). In Thm 1.2's proof: k=3 (=> 2^{38}), Lambda_n = a log(-1) + log(b_2/b_1) + n log(alpha_2/alpha_1) with |a|<=n+2; choices A_1 = pi, A_2 = 2B(u)+pi, A_3 = 2 log I(f*), D_1 <= m! dD; the extra factor 2^2 (giving 2^{40} in C(u)) comes from A_3 = 2 log I and log(en+2e) <= 2 log n.
Proof mechanics worth reusing: (i) e^{-B(u)} <= |b_j| <= e^{B(u)} (his eq:|bj|), derived from |log|b_j|| <= [Q(b_j):Q] h(b_j) <= m! dD h(b_j); (ii) Thm 1.1 endgame: n > (2B+log m)/(-log(1 - J/|alpha_1|)) suffices, then |alpha_1| < 1+H(f) (Cauchy, Lemma 2.2) and -log(1-x) > x; (iii) Thm 1.2 endgame: need n J/I - C log n >= 2B + log(2m) (his eq:n1), solved by n >= 4 C I J^{-1} log(2 C I J^{-1}) via x >= 2A log A => x/log x >= A; Lambda_n = 0 subcases give n <= 4B(u) (non-unit ratio, p-adic) or handled by n > 4 B I J^{-1} (unit ratio, his eq:n02); (iv) two-max-root lower bound: |b_1 alpha_1^n + b_2 alpha_2^n| > (1/2)|b_1 alpha_1^n| exp(-C(u) log n) (his eq:b1b2).

== APPLICABILITY CONDITIONS (adversarial audit) ==
(a) SIMPLE required in both theorems (k=m, distinct roots). (b) Full non-degeneracy NOT required: Thm 1.1 needs no root-of-unity condition at all; Thm 1.2 needs ONLY alpha_1/alpha_2 (quotient of the two maximal-modulus roots) not a root of unity. (c) a_0 != 0 required (def. of LRS). (d) At least one initial term nonzero. (e) IMPLICIT HYPOTHESIS not in the theorem statements: both proofs and Lemma 2.8 write u_n = sum b_j alpha_j^n "where b_1,...,b_m are all non-zero" — i.e., the recurrence must be minimal for u (no Binet coefficient vanishes). Must be verified separately. (f) Bounds are for n >= 0 only (forward); negative indices need sequence reversal. (g) Fully explicit/effective: only ingredients are Mahler-type root separation, Cauchy's bound, and Matveev's theorem; no ineffective steps. (h) Thm 1.2 has no "real polynomial" improvement (uses complex-pair separation eq:udis1 anyway). (i) d is the Galois-closure degree of the field generated by the RECURRENCE COEFFICIENTS, not by the roots — coefficients in Q give d=1 even though the splitting field is bigger.
mapped_to_our_setting:
== DICTIONARY (our objects -> Sha's data) ==
K = Q(alpha), alpha = d^{1/3} (d>=2 cube-free), complex cubic; sigma1 real, sigma2/sigma3 complex pair; eps fundamental unit, lambda = sigma1(eps), |lambda| = e^R > 1, mu = sigma2(eps), |mu| = e^{-R/2}. Since [K:Q]=3 is prime and eps is not a root of unity, Q(eps)=K, so the char poly of eps, f(X) = X^3 - t X^2 + s X - nu with t = Tr(eps) in Z, s = e_2(lambda,mu,mubar) in Z, nu = N(eps) = +-1, is the (irreducible, hence squarefree) minimal polynomial; roots lambda, mu, mubar distinct. With omega = e^{2pi i/3}: sigma1(g) + omega sigma2(g) + omega^2 sigma3(g) = 3 d^{2/3} z(g) where z(g) is the alpha^2-coordinate of g. So the integer-valued sequence is z_k := c(k)/(3 d^{2/3}) in Z (times 3 if O_K != Z[alpha]; see gaps), with Binet form z_k = b_1 lambda^k + b_2 mu^k + b_3 mubar^k, b_1 = sigma1(gamma)/(3d^{2/3}), b_2 = omega sigma2(gamma)/(3d^{2/3}), b_3 = conj(b_2). All b_j != 0 iff gamma != 0 — condition (e) holds.
CRUCIAL PARAMETER COLLAPSE: coefficients of the recurrence are RATIONAL INTEGERS => Sha's d = 1 (Galois closure of Q(a_0,a_1,a_2) = Q), delta_f = 1, f* = f; initial terms z_0,z_1,z_2 in Z => D = 1; m = 3, m! dD = 6. (Do NOT feed Sha the K-valued or omega-weighted form: coefficients generating K would give d = 6, dm = 18, J ~ I^{-2594}.)
Useful identities: h(eps) = R/3 (M(minpoly eps) = |lambda| = e^R, degree 3); h(a_2*) = log|t| <= R + log 3; h(a_1*) = log|s| <= R/2 + log 3; h(a_3*) = h(a_0*) = 0. So S_f := sum_{i=0}^3 h(a_i*) <= (3/2)R + 2 log 3 = (9/2)h(eps) + 2 log 3.

== FORWARD DIRECTION (k -> +infty): Theorem 1.1, real-polynomial variant ==
Hypotheses: simple (roots distinct, above); dominant root lambda (|lambda| = e^R > e^{-R/2} = |mu| for R > 0); a_0 = -nu = -+1 != 0; z_0=z_1=z_2=0 forces gamma=0 (invertible Vandermonde, b_j != 0). No non-degeneracy needed. f real (integer) => improved J.
Specialized constants (d=1, m=3, dm=3):
  I(f*) = 2^3 * 4^{1/2} * e^{S_f} = 16 e^{S_f} <= 144 e^{3R/2};  log I(f*) <= 1.5R + 4.97.
  J_real = 2^{-3*2*1/2} * 4^{-3*2-1/2} * I^{-2*3*2-1} = 2^{-16} I(f*)^{-13}  [>= 2^{-16}(144)^{-13} e^{-19.5R}].
  (General J(f*) = 2^{3/2} * 4^{-11.5} * I^{-14} = 2^{-21.5} I(f*)^{-14}, not needed forward.)
  B(u) = 6( sum_{i=0}^2 h(z_i) + 27 S_f + 27 log 4 ). With log(house gamma) <= 3 h(gamma) and |z_i| <= house(gamma) e^{iR} (i=0,1,2; the d^{-2/3} <= 1 dropped): sum h(z_i) <= 9 h(gamma) + 3R, so B(u) <= 54 h(gamma) + 261 R + 581.
  H(f) = max(1,|t|,|s|) <= e^R + 2.
  N_1 = (2B(u) + log 3)(1 + H(f)) 2^{16} I(f*)^{13} <= (108 h(gamma) + 522 R + 1163) * (e^R + 3) * 2^{16} * 144^{13} e^{19.5R}
      <= 2 * 10^{36} (h(gamma) + R + 1) e^{20.5 R}   (R >= 1).
So c(k) != 0 for all k > N_1 (forward, base index k = 0).

== BACKWARD DIRECTION (k -> -infty): Theorem 1.2 on the reversed sequence ==
Reversal: v_n := z_{-n} satisfies the order-3 recurrence with char poly g(X) = char poly of eps^{-1} = X^3 - s' X^2 + t' X - nu', s' = Tr(eps^{-1}) in Z (|s'| <= 2e^{R/2}+e^{-R}), t' = +-t in Z (|t'| <= e^R + 2), nu' = +-1. Coefficients stay in Z because a_0 = +-1 (eps is a unit). Roots: 1/lambda (modulus e^{-R}), 1/mu, 1/mubar (modulus e^{R/2}).
Hypothesis audit for Thm 1.2: (i) simple: distinct roots (inverses of distinct). (ii) EXACTLY TWO roots of maximal modulus: |1/mu| = |1/mubar| = e^{R/2} > e^{-R} = |1/lambda| iff R > 0 — yes, a genuine conjugate complex pair with the third root strictly smaller; Sha's theorem is stated for any two maximal-modulus roots and his proof uses only |alpha_1| = |alpha_2| > |alpha_j| (j>=3) plus Matveev — a conjugate pair is the archetypal case (the ratio alpha_2/alpha_1 has modulus 1, |log(alpha_2/alpha_1)| <= pi as he uses). (iii) quotient not a root of unity: (1/mu)/(1/mubar) = mubar/mu. PROOF: if (mubar/mu)^t = 1, t >= 1, then sigma2(eps^t) = sigma3(eps^t) = conj(sigma2(eps^t)), so sigma2(eps^t) is real; since [K:Q] = 3 is prime, either eps^t in Q — then eps^t is a rational unit = +-1, contradicting |sigma1(eps^t)| = e^{tR} > 1 — or Q(eps^t) = K, in which case the three conjugates of eps^t are pairwise distinct, contradicting sigma2 = sigma3. Hence mubar/mu is not a root of unity. (Same argument gives full non-degeneracy: |lambda/mu| = e^{3R/2} != 1 kills the other ratios; so the LRS is non-degenerate and Skolem-Mahler-Lech zero set is finite — but Thm 1.2 only needs the mubar/mu condition.) (iv) a_0 = -nu' != 0; (v) b_j != 0 as before; (vi) m = 3 >= 3, consistent with Sha's remark.
Specialized constants (d=1, D=1, m=3): sum h(g_i*) <= (3/2)R + 2 log 3 = S_f again, so I(g*) <= 144 e^{3R/2}; J(g*) = 2^{-21.5} I(g*)^{-14} (general complex form — mandatory here, the maximal pair is non-real); I(g*) J(g*)^{-1} = 2^{21.5} I(g*)^{15} <= 2^{21.5} 144^{15} e^{22.5R} <= 7.2 * 10^{38} e^{22.5R}.
  Initial terms v_i = z_{-i}, i=0,1,2: |z_{-i}| <= house(gamma) e^{iR/2} => sum h(v_i) <= 9 h(gamma) + 1.5 R => B(v) <= 54 h(gamma) + 252 R + 581.
  C(v) = 2^{40} * 36 * pi * (2B(v) + pi) * log I(g*) * log(6e) <= 3.48 * 10^{14} (2B(v)+pi)(1.5R+5).
  N_2 = 4 C(v) I(g*) J(g*)^{-1} log(2 C(v) I(g*) J(g*)^{-1});
  crude closed form: N_2 <= 10^{60} (h(gamma)+R+2)^2 (R+1)^2 e^{22.5 R}.
So c(k) != 0 for all k < -N_2 (base index 0); combined zero window: every zero k of c satisfies -N_2 <= k <= N_1.

== HEIGHTS OF THE ALGEBRAIC (BINET) COEFFICIENTS ==
Sha's theorems do NOT take h(b_j) as input — inputs are h(initial terms) and h(recurrence coefficients); h(b_j) is bounded internally by Lemma 2.8: h(b_j) < sum_{i=0}^2 h(z_i) + 27 S_f + 27 log 4 = B(u)/6 (<= 9h(gamma) + 43.5R + 97 forward). Direct computation is far better: h(sigma_j(gamma)) = h(gamma) (Weil height is Galois-invariant), h(omega) = 0, h(3 d^{2/3}) = log 3 + (2/3) log d, so
  h(b_j) <= h(gamma) + log 3 + (2/3) log d,   [Q(b_j):Q] <= 6 (b_j in Q(alpha, omega), degree 6).
Since Sha's proofs use B(u) only through |log|b_j|| <= [Q(b_j):Q] h(b_j) (his eq:|bj|) and |ord_p(b_j)| <= 2B (his eq:ord2), one may legitimately rerun his proof with B' := 6(h(gamma) + log 3 + (2/3) log d) in place of B(u) — this is a (safe) modification of the black-box statement, improving the poly factor but NOT the e^{Theta(R)} factor, which comes from J. Similarly, direct A_2 = 12 h(gamma) + pi (h(b_2/b_1) <= 2h(gamma)) and A_3 = 6*(2R/3) = 4R (h(mubar/mu) <= 2h(eps) = 2R/3) beat Sha's generic choices.

== WINDOW SHAPE W(s) ==
With s := max(R, h(gamma), log|m|, log d) (any instance-size measure dominating these; note for a unit-reduced representative gamma one can arrange log house(gamma) <= (1/3)log|m| + O(R), hence h(gamma) <= (1/3)log|m| + R + O(1) — our lemma, not Sha's):
  W_black-box = N_1 + N_2 <= 10^{61} (h(gamma)+R+2)^2 (R+1)^2 e^{22.5 R}  = 2^{32.5 s + O(log s)} = 2^{O(s)}.
ANSWER: Sha's theorems as stated give W = 2^{O(s)} — the requirement W = 2^{O(s)} IS met (explicit exponent: e^{20.5R} forward via I^{13}*(1+H), e^{22.5R} backward via I^{15}); poly(s) is NOT what the theorem statements give. The exponential is entirely an artifact of the worst-case root-modulus separation J(f*) ~ I^{-14} (Mahler/discriminant bound, Lemmas 2.5/2.7) and the Cauchy bound |alpha_1| < 1+H(f): in our instance the TRUE data are |alpha_1| = e^R (resp. e^{R/2}) and the ratio gap -log(|alpha_3|/|alpha_1|) = 3R/2 exactly (both directions), i.e., 1 - |alpha_3|/|alpha_1| = 1 - e^{-3R/2} ~ 1, exponentially LARGER than J/|alpha_1|. Substituting the true gap into Sha's own endgame inequalities (his eq:b1 / eq:n1) — a one-line change of his proofs, not of his theorems — gives:
  forward: k_+ <= (2B + log 3)/(3R/2) = O(h(gamma)/R + 1);
  backward: need n(3R/2) - C(v) log n >= 2B(v) + log 6, giving k_- <= max( (8C(v)/(3R)) log(4C(v)/(3R)), (4/(3R))(2B(v)+log 6) ) = O((h(gamma)+R) log(h(gamma)+R+2)) since C(v)/R = O(h(gamma)+R) (and O((h(gamma)+log d) log(...)) with the direct B', A_2, A_3 above);
i.e., W = poly(s) (quasi-linear) is achievable from Sha's METHOD with our explicit separation, but it must be written as our own theorem citing his Lemma 2.8 + Matveev step, not quoted from his Theorems 1.1/1.2.
gaps:
1. NEGATIVE INDICES: Sha bounds zeros only for n >= 0. The k -> -infty direction requires the reversal v_n = u_{-n}; legitimate here because a_0 = +-N(eps) = +-1 keeps the reversed recurrence integral (coefficients = +-elementary symmetric functions of 1/lambda, 1/mu, 1/mubar in Z). The reversal itself and the two-sided assembly are ours, not in the paper.
2. IMPLICIT HYPOTHESIS: "b_1,...,b_m all non-zero" appears only inside Lemma 2.8 and the proofs, not in the statements of Theorems 1.1/1.2. If a Binet coefficient of the dominant/maximal roots vanished, the proofs would break. We must (and do) verify b_j != 0 (gamma != 0, omega != 0) in the write-up.
3. EXPONENTIAL LOSSINESS: Sha provides no mechanism to exploit a KNOWN root separation; J(f*) is the worst-case Mahler-type bound, e^{-Theta(R)} for us, though the true modulus gap is e^{R/2} - e^{-R} (huge). Consequently his Theorems give only W = 2^{O(s)}. The poly(s) window requires re-deriving his final inequalities with the exact ratio gap 3R/2 — all ingredients (Matveev application with A_1 = pi, A_2, A_3; the Lambda_n = 0 subcases via p-adic ord / unit-conjugate argument; Lemma 2.8) are in the paper, but the resulting statement is not quotable as his theorem.
4. CHOICE OF REPRESENTATIVE: Sha says nothing about heights of gamma; the reduction h(gamma) <= (1/3)log|m| + R + O(1) (unit-lattice / Minkowski reduction along the norm-m orbit) is entirely our job, as is the relation R = 3 h(eps).
5. INTEGRALITY DETAILS: if O_K != Z[alpha] (possible for d ≡ +-1 mod 9, index 3), z_k may lie in (1/3)Z; replace z_k by 3 z_k (same recurrence; heights shift by <= log 3). Also if gamma is not integral, clear denominators first. Sha's theorems allow algebraic u_i anyway (then D <= 3), but the d = D = 1 rational-integer normalization is what keeps the constants tame; the paper does not warn that feeding K-rational coefficients inflates d to 6 and J to I^{-2594}.
6. Sha's C(u) rests on Matveev 2000 (Cor. 2.3) with k = 3 logs (2^{38} -> 2^{40}); he explicitly remarks (Sec. 2.4) that sharper 3-log bounds (Mignotte's kit) have side conditions that need not hold — no improvement available off the shelf.
7. The theorems give only "u_n != 0 for n > N": locating/deciding zeros inside [−N_2, N_1] (a 2^{O(s)}-size search under the black-box bounds) is outside the paper; likewise the arithmetic-progression part of Skolem–Mahler–Lech (irrelevant for us: the sequence is non-degenerate, zero set finite; Berstel–Mignotte cited by Sha for the AP part).
8. Minor: heights of zero initial terms are undefined (h(0)); harmless — Lemma 2.8 only needs upper bounds and Sha requires at least one u_j != 0 (for us all-zero initial block forces gamma = 0).
9. Theorem numbering caveat: numbers quoted (1.1, 1.2, Lemmas 2.5–2.8) are from arXiv v6 (2 Oct 2018), the accepted version of J. Number Theory 197 (2019) 228–249; the published PDF was not independently checked page-by-page, but v6 is the "to appear" text and the numbering scheme (amsart, per-section) matches.

citations.ref: Min Sha, Effective results on the Skolem Problem for linear recurrence sequences, arXiv:1505.07147v6 (full LaTeX source; Theorems 1.1, 1.2; Lemmas 2.5-2.8; proofs Secs. 3-4)

findings:
## A. Schoof, "Computing Arakelov class groups", MSRI Publ. 44 (2008), pp. 447-495 [SCH]

Sec. 10 (p. 479): "We view the degree n of F as fixed and estimate the running times of the algorithms in terms of Delta_F." Reduced divisor D=d(I)=(I, N(I)^{-1/n}) "can be represented using only (log|Delta_F|)^{O(n)} bits" (I^{-1} integral of norm <= dF := (2/pi)^{r2} |Delta_F|^{1/2}).

* PROP 7.1: for reduced d(I), ||1||_D <= sqrt(n)||x||_D for all nonzero x in I (1 is within sqrt(n) of shortest).
* PROP 7.2: (i) if d(I) reduced then I^{-1} is an integral O_F-ideal of norm <= dF; (ii) Red_F is finite; (iii) Red_F -> oriented Picard group ~Pic^0_F is injective.
* THM 7.7 (used via Cor 7.8): balls of radius (1/2)log(4/3) centered at reduced divisors are mutually disjoint in ~Pic^0_F. COR 7.8: number of reduced divisors in a ball of radius 1 in Pic^0_F is <= (cn)^{n/2} (absolute c). COR 7.9: #Red_F <= vol(~Pic^0_F) * 6^n. COR 7.10: (log|Delta_F|)^{-n} <= #Red_F / vol(Pic^0_F) <= 18^n. PROP 7.11(i): vol(Pic^0_F) <= sqrt(|Delta_F|) (log|Delta_F|)^{n-1} (unconditional).
* ALG 10.3 (Reduction): given D=(I,u) in Div^0, checks reducedness and computes a reduced divisor D' with ||D-D'||_Pic <= log dF; "Both algorithms run in time polynomial in log|Delta_F|, log||u|| and the logarithmic height of N(I)." (Shortest vector computed exactly: LLL then enumeration of boundedly many integer coordinates, Cor 10.2 — coordinate bound independent of Delta; deterministic.)
* ALG 10.4 (Composition), ALG 10.5 (Inversion): polytime in log|Delta_F| for reduced inputs.
* ALG 10.7 (Scan): computes ALL reduced divisors in a ball of radius 1 around a given degree-0 divisor, "in time polynomial in log|Delta_F|" (per ball; via Lemma 10.6, minimal elements mu with ||mu||_P <= e^{2eps} sqrt(n) x shortest).
* ALG 10.8 (Jump): given a divisor D = sum n_p p + sum x_sigma sigma of degree 0 with O(log|Delta_F|) nonzero coefficients of size |Delta_F|^{O(1)}, computes a reduced divisor at distance < log dF from D in polytime, by BINARY DOUBLING (t in O(log|Delta_F|) doublings, w^{2^t}=v); each doubling is a composition+reduction producing an explicit relative element x (I_{i+1} = I_i^2/(x)).
* Sec. 11 header: "In this section we describe a deterministic algorithm to compute the Arakelov class group of a number field F of degree n and discriminant Delta_F. It runs in time proportional to sqrt(|Delta_F|) times a power of log|Delta_F|."
* LEMMA 11.1: any ideal J subset O_F with N(J) < B is J = x I^{-1} with (I, N(I)^{-1/n}) reduced, ||u||_Pic < log dF, x in I, ||x||_{D+(O_F,u)} < sqrt(n) B^{1/n}. (Note: the text of Alg 11.2's analysis cites "Lemma 11.2"; this is a typo for Lemma 11.1.)
* ALG 11.2: given all reduced divisors in a connected component of Pic^0_F, detect all divisors of the form (J^{-1}, N(J)^{1/n}) with J subset O_F, N(J) < dF, on that component; analysis: number of ideals J is <= sqrt(|Delta_F|) polylog (citing [Lenstra 1992, Thm 6.5]); each ideal found at most polylog(|Delta_F|) times (Dobrowolski height bound kills unit collisions); work proportional to component volume x polylog.
* Sec. 11, "A deterministic algorithm" (pp. 487-489): VERBATIM: "Finally we explain the deterministic algorithm to compute the Arakelov class group of a number field F. This algorithm seems to have been known to the experts. It was explained to me by Hendrik Lenstra." Procedure: scan identity component with Alg 10.3 (balls of radius 2 log dF) obtaining ALL reduced divisors on the component of identity and "the absolute values of a set of generators of the unit group O_F^*"; "The running time is proportional to the volume of the connected component of identity and is polynomial in log|Delta_F|." Then Alg 11.2 builds list L of all integral J, N(J) <= dF, whose divisor lies on the scanned components; Minkowski primes not in L are found by factoring the defining polynomial mod p "with a simple minded trial division algorithm, the amount of work is at most p^i < dF times a power of log|Delta_F|"; at most log|Delta_F| new class generators; total: "this algorithm takes time at most sqrt(|Delta_F|) times power of log|Delta_F|." Output = full Arakelov class group: class group AND regulator/log-unit data. NO explicit principal-ideal-problem theorem is stated; no generator tracking is performed.

## B. Lenstra, "Algorithms in algebraic number theory", Bull. AMS 26 (1992) 211-244 [LEN]

* Sec. 5 (context for Problem 5.1): VERBATIM: "Once the class group has been calculated in this sense, it may remain very difficult to find the inverse isomorphism: given an O-ideal, to which ideal of the form prod a_i^{m(i)} is it equivalent? Even testing whether a given ideal is principal may be very difficult." (i.e., possessing class-group data does NOT by itself give a PIP algorithm.)
* THM 5.4: with d=(2/pi)^s |Delta|^{1/2}, S = S_inf ∪ {p : Np <= d}: K_S is generated by x with H(x) <= d^2, and Cl(O) is generated by classes of finite primes in S. (Effective computability of O^* and Cl O follows; exact sequence 0 -> O^* -> K_S -> Z^{S-S_inf} -> Cl O -> 0.)
* THM 5.5 VERBATIM: "Given K and O, one can determine a set of generators of O^* and the structure of Cl O in time at most (2+log|Delta|)^{O(n)} |Delta|^{3/4} by means of a deterministic algorithm and in expected time at most (2+log|Delta|)^{O(n)} |Delta|^{1/2} by means of a probabilistic algorithm." Preceded by: "The following theorem appears to be true." Followed by: "In [12] one finds a weaker version of this result, in which n is kept fixed. The more precise result should follow by combining [12] with results that appear in [15]." Here [12] = J. Buchmann, "Complexity of algorithms in algebraic number theory", Proc. First Conf. Canadian Number Theory Assoc. (Banff 1988), de Gruyter 1990, 37-53; [15] = Buchmann-Shoup. Also VERBATIM correction of [12]: "note that, in that paper, R^{1/2} D^eps in Theorem 2 is a printing error for R D^eps, and D^{1/2+eps} in Theorem 4 is a printing error for R^{1/2} D^eps". And: "The higher exponent 3/4 in Theorem 5.5 in the case of a deterministic algorithm is due to the use of algorithms for factoring polynomials over finite fields (see 2.8)." PROBLEM 5.6: "Can the exponent 3/4 in Theorem 5.5 be replaced by 1/2?" (affirmative for quadratic fields).
* THM 6.5 VERBATIM: "Let ... d=(2/pi)^s|Delta|^{1/2}. Then the class number h and the regulator R of K satisfy h <= d (n-1+log d)^{n-1}/(n-1)!, and hR <= d (log d)^{n-1-s} (n-1+log d)^s /(n-1)!." In its PROOF, VERBATIM: "For each positive integer m, the number of O-ideals of norm m is at most the number of vectors x=(x_i)_{i=1}^n in Z_{>0}^n satisfying prod_i x_i = m. One proves this by considering how rational primes can split in K." (i.e., #{ideals of norm m} <= d_n(m), the n-fold divisor function.)

## C. Buchmann-Williams, "On the infrastructure of the principal ideal class of an algebraic number field of unit rank one", Math. Comp. 50 (1988) 569-579 [BW88] — applies to ANY order O of unit rank 1: real quadratic, COMPLEX CUBIC, totally complex quartic; D = |disc(O)|, R = regulator.

* PROP 2.4: any minimum mu of ideal a satisfies |N(mu)| <= sqrt(D) N(a).
* PROP 2.6: the ordered sequence (lambda_i) = Log of minima of O is purely periodic modulo R.
* PROP 2.7: (i) lambda_{k+1}-lambda_k < log sqrt(D) for all k [Buchmann, J. Number Theory 26 (1987) 31-37]; (ii) lambda_{k+j}-lambda_k > c1 with (j,c1) = (2, log 2) for n=2, (7, log 4) for n=3 [Williams, "The spacing of the minima in certain cubic lattices", Pacific J. Math. 124 (1986) 483-496], (30, log(2cos(pi/5))) for n=4. [Caveat: "log 4" for n=3 read from a scanned PDF; verify against Williams 1986. Williams' spacing theorem is proved for lattices of complex cubic orders.]
* COR 2.8: cycle length p (number of reduced principal ideals = minima mod units) satisfies R/log sqrt(D) < p <= jR/c1. So p = Theta~(R).
* PROP 2.11: a minimum mu is represented by phi(mu) = ((1/mu)O as HNF A, denominator d) with d < sqrt(D), |A|_inf < sqrt(D) — poly-size; whereas VERBATIM "the order of magnitude of a minimum can be as large as exp(sqrt(D)) (see Patterson and Williams)" — so generators MUST NOT be output in coordinates.
* ALG 2.13 + PROP 2.14: baby-step computation of R in O(R D^eps) binary operations on numbers of size O(D^eps); per-step neighbor-minimum computation (Voronoi-type) costs O(D^eps), cited to Williams (Rocky Mountain J. Math. 15 (1985) 621-655) for n=3 and Buchmann (Math. Comp. 48 (1987) 39-54) for n=4.
* THM 3.3: baby-step/giant-step computes R in O(R^{1/2} D^eps) binary operations.
* DEF 4.1/PROP 4.2: a reduced iff 1 is a minimum of a; (1/mu)a is reduced for any minimum mu of a (reduction is one shortest/minimum computation).
* PROP 4.3: a reduced ideal a is principal iff there is a minimum mu of O with phi_1(mu)=a and 0 <= phi_2(mu) < R. [proof cited to Buchmann, J. Number Theory 26 (1987) 8-30, Thm 6.2.]
* ALG 4.4 (baby-step principal ideal test): Step 1 compute ALL reduced principal ideals (the cycle), store HNFs sorted; Step 2 reduce input a to a* = (1/mu)a; Step 3 a principal iff a* is in the stored list. PROP 4.5 VERBATIM: "Let a be a fractional ideal of O. Algorithm 4.4 tests a for principality in O(log(|HNF(a)|_inf d(a)) + D^eps log(d(a)^n N(a)) + R D^eps) binary operations."
* ALG 4.6 (giant-step principal ideal test; baby stock up to sqrt(R)+c4+c5+log sqrt(D), K=ceil(sqrt(R)) giant steps). THM 4.7 VERBATIM: "Let a be a fractional ideal of O. Algorithm 4.6 tests a for principality in O(log(|HNF(a)|_inf d(a)) + D^eps log(d(a)^n N(a)) + R^{1/2} D^eps) binary operations." Since R <= hR <= D^{1/2} polylog (LEN Thm 6.5), these are D^{1/2+o(1)} resp. D^{1/4+o(1)} deterministic. DECISION only as stated; generator implicit (see [BW87] next).

## D. Buchmann-Williams, "On principal ideal testing in algebraic number fields", J. Symbolic Comput. 4 (1987) 11-19 [BW87] (general title, rank-1 mechanism): Algorithm 1.1: Step 1 computes "the cycle I = {(alpha_1),...,(alpha_rho)} of all reduced principal ideals in O_F" (cycle computation delegated to Buchmann 1987); Step 3: "The ideal a is principal if and only if a' in I, i.e. a = (mu alpha_k) for some k" — i.e., the GENERATOR is recovered as mu*alpha_k (mu = the minimum used to reduce a; alpha_k = the cycle generator at the matching position). alpha_k is only implicitly represented by its position/HNF (it has exp(sqrt(D)) coordinates in general). Minimum computation: "mu can be computed in O(log(||B|| D)) binary operations" via shortest vector in a rational lattice approximation with precision chosen so the output is provably a minimum.

## E. Thiel [THI]

* C. Thiel, "Under the assumption of the Generalized Riemann Hypothesis verifying the class number belongs to NP ∩ co-NP", ANTS-I, LNCS 877 (1994): abstract states it introduces "a compact representation of algebraic integers which allows representation of a system of fundamental units by (2+log_2 Delta)^{O(1)} bits".
* C. Thiel, "Short proofs using compact representations of algebraic integers", J. Complexity 11 (1995) 310-329; and PhD thesis "On the complexity of some problems in algorithmic algebraic number theory", Saarbruecken 1995. Fontein-Jacobson (arXiv:1001.4187) cite [Thi95, Cor 3.7]: "one can represent a reduced ideal with at most ([K:Q]^2+1) log_2 sqrt(Delta_K) bits". Biasse-Fieker (LMS JCM 17A (2014), Sec. 5) define compact representation (rewrite beta = prod_j gamma_j^{l^j}, gamma_j of polynomial size, polynomially many terms) and state VERBATIM: "An exponential algorithm was presented in [35]" ([35] = Thiel's thesis) — i.e., Thiel's thesis contains an (exponential-time, unconditional) algorithm computing compact representations in arbitrary degree; Biasse-Fieker Alg. 7 + Prop 5.1 give a subexponential variant. Eisentraeger-Hallgren (ANTS-X, OBS 1 (2013) 17-34) confirm: Thiel "used compact representations in number fields and showed [the class number verification and] the computation of compact representations of units are in NP ∩ coNP for number fields" and that compact representations put the principal ideal problem in NP (their paper does the function-field analogue; Def 4.3, Alg 4.6 'Compact representation' of a generator of an ideal).

## F. Biasse-Fieker, "Subexponential class group and unit group computation in large degree number fields", LMS J. Comput. Math. 17A (2014) 385-403 [BF14]: THM 6.1 (GRH + Heuristics 1,3): class group + compact representation of a fundamental system of units in time L_Delta(a,c), a=2/3+eps general, a=1/2 for n <= log^{3/4-eps}|Delta|. THM 6.2 (GRH + Heuristics 2,3): L(a) tradeoffs. All HEURISTIC + GRH; no deterministic claim; no explicit PIP theorem in this paper (their PIP algorithms are in Biasse-Fieker ANTS/arXiv:1204.1294 and Biasse arXiv:1204.1292, equally heuristic). Relevant to us only for the compact-representation formalism and the Thiel attribution.

## G. Context (not needed for the deterministic claim): Biasse-Song (SODA 2016, quoted in arXiv:2405.12508 Thm 2.4): quantum poly-time S-unit/PIP with compact-representation output, arbitrary degree.
mapped_to_our_setting:
Setting: K cubic (n=3 fixed), s = input bit size, |Delta_K| = 2^{O(s)}, |m| = 2^{O(s)}; certified bnf given: integral basis of O_K, Cl_K structure, fundamental units in compact representation with certified log-embeddings, regulator R (certified approximation). dF = (2/pi)^{r2}|Delta|^{1/2}. From LEN Thm 6.5 (n=3): hR <= (2/pi)^{r2}|Delta|^{1/2} (log d)(2+log d)/2 = |Delta|^{1/2} poly(s), so R = 2^{O(s)} and h = 2^{O(s)}.

STEP 1 (factor m): trial division up to |m|^{1/2}: deterministic 2^{s/2} poly(s) = 2^{O(s)}. No citation needed.

STEP 2 (ideals of norm |m|): (a) primes above p | m: maximal ideals of the 3-dimensional F_p-algebra O_K/pO_K; deterministically: factor the defining polynomial mod p by trial division (cost < p poly, exactly as Schoof Sec. 11 does: "simple minded trial division... at most p^i times a power of log|Delta|"), or fully elementarily enumerate candidate F_p-subspaces (p^{O(n^2)} = 2^{O(s)}); residue degrees f_i from N(p_i) = |O_K/p_i|. (b) count: ideals of norm p^a correspond to exponent vectors (e_1..e_g), sum e_i f_i = a, g <= 3, so <= (a+1)^3 per prime; total #{ideals of norm |m|} <= prod_{p^a || m}(a+1)^3 = d(|m|)^3 <= 8|m|^{3/2} = 2^{O(s)} (in fact <= d_3(|m|) = |m|^{o(1)}; citable: LEN Thm 6.5 proof line "#{O-ideals of norm m} <= #{x in Z_{>0}^n : prod x_i = m}"). Each candidate ideal assembled by ideal multiplication in HNF: poly(s) each.

STEP 3 (PIP per candidate ideal I, N(I) = |m|):
- COMPLEX CUBIC (disc < 0, unit rank 1) — explicit theorems exist: BW88 Prop 4.5: deterministic O(log(|HNF(I)|_inf d(I)) + D^eps log(d(I)^3 N(I)) + R D^eps) = |Delta|^{1/2+o(1)} + poly(s) bit ops (baby-step; cycle length p <= 7R/log 4 + O(1) by Cor 2.8/Prop 2.7); BW88 Thm 4.7: O(... + R^{1/2} D^eps) = |Delta|^{1/4+o(1)} + poly(s) (giant-step, decision). Both <= 2^{O(s)}. Generator: BW87 Alg 1.1 Step 3 gives gamma = mu * alpha_k (mu = reduction minimum of I, poly-size by Prop 2.11 mechanism; alpha_k = generator of the k-th reduced principal ideal). alpha_k in COMPACT representation: run Schoof Alg 10.8-style doubling to position lambda_k (t = O(log lambda_k) = O(s) doublings; each doubling contributes one explicit relative element x_i of poly(s) size since all intermediate ideals are reduced with N(I_i^{-1}) <= dF), yielding alpha_k = prod x_i^{2^{t-i}} — a compact representation with O(s) factors, poly(s) bits each; alternatively cite Thiel's thesis (exponential-time compact-representation algorithm, per BF14 Sec. 5) — exponential is within budget.
- GENERAL fixed degree incl. TOTALLY REAL CUBIC (rank 2): assemble from SCH: reduce (I, N(I)^{-1/3}) by Alg 10.3 obtaining minimal f in I and reduced J = f^{-1}I (so I = (f)J, f poly-size); scan the identity component of Pic^0 (Sec. 11 procedure: balls of radius 2 log dF via Alg 10.3/10.7, cost = vol(identity component) x poly(s) <= vol(Pic^0) poly(s) <= |Delta|^{1/2} poly(s) by Prop 7.11(i)); I principal iff the reduced divisor d(J) appears in the scanned list (membership by exact HNF comparison; d(J) determined by J alone; injectivity Prop 7.2(iii) in ~Pic^0 — work in the oriented group); component count of reduced divisors <= 18^n vol (Cor 7.9/7.10) = 2^{O(s)}; generator by tracking the relative elements along the walk, compacted by the same doubling trick. Total: deterministic |Delta|^{1/2+o(1)} + poly-in-input = 2^{O(s)}.

TOTAL: 2^{O(s)} x d(|m|)^3 PIP calls = 2^{O(s)} deterministic. If only the complex cubic case is needed, everything rests on BW88/BW87 + LEN Thm 6.5.

ORBIT ACCOUNTING (odd degree): N((gamma)) = |N(gamma)|; deg 3 odd => N(-gamma) = -N(gamma), so every principal ideal of norm |m| has a generator of norm exactly m. For fixed gamma_0 with N(gamma_0) = m, {generators with norm m} = gamma_0 * U^+ where U^+ = {u in O_K^* : N(u) = 1}; and O_K^*-orbits of {N = m} = U^+-orbits = principal ideals of norm |m| (bijection). So: output one compact-rep generator (sign-corrected) per principal candidate ideal; distinct ideals are exactly distinct orbits; deduplication is exact HNF ideal equality — no log comparison needed. (Whether U^+ = O_K^* or index 2: compute N(epsilon) in {+-1} exactly from the compact representation via N(prod gamma_i^{2^i}) = prod N(gamma_i)^{2^i}, rational arithmetic; irrelevant to the count but needed if one wants representatives modulo U^+ specifically.)

ANSWERS: (a) YES: given certified bnf, PIP for fixed degree is deterministically solvable in |Delta|^{1/2+o(1)} + poly(size of I) bit operations with the generator in compact representation; rank 1 admits the better decision bound |Delta|^{1/4+o(1)} (BW88 Thm 4.7). All unconditional (no GRH: GRH enters only the subexponential/heuristic literature and Bach bounds, neither used). (b) NOT stated as a single citable theorem anywhere in these primary sources. Explicitly stated pieces: BW88 Prop 4.5 + Thm 4.7 (rank-1 PIP decision, exact complexity, verbatim above); BW87 Alg 1.1 (rank-1 generator recovery, no compact rep); SCH Sec. 11 (deterministic sqrt(|Delta|) polylog Arakelov class group — with Schoof's own attribution "known to the experts... explained to me by Hendrik Lenstra", i.e., folklore made semi-explicit; no PIP statement, no generator tracking); LEN Thm 5.5 (bnf computation D^{3/4} deterministic / D^{1/2} probabilistic, fixed n from Buchmann's Banff paper, and Lenstra says "appears to be true" — not even fully claimed as proved for varying n); THI (compact representations exist, poly bits, exponential-time computable — thesis/J.Complexity, exact statements not retrievable online). The assembled lemma is therefore folklore built from: lattice/infrastructure enumeration in a rank-<=2 log-unit torus (constant dimension) + compact representations.

RANK-1 CONCRETE SKETCH (complex cubic; what "1-dimensional log-unit lattice" buys): Log M_O (logs |mu|_1 of minima of O_K) is a discrete subset of R, purely periodic mod R (BW88 Prop 2.6), totally ordered — a single CYCLE of reduced principal ideals (1/mu)O_K, length p in (R/log sqrt(D), 7R/log 4] (Cor 2.8). Principality test = reduce I once (one minimum computation, LLL + bounded exact enumeration), then membership of the resulting reduced ideal in the cycle: walk the cycle by Voronoi neighbor steps (each deterministic poly(s): candidates from LLL basis with absolutely bounded integer coordinates, Schoof Cor 10.2), compare HNFs exactly. The 1-dimensionality makes the infrastructure a cycle with a canonical successor (no CVP, no web/mesh as in rank 2), termination exact (return to O_K), ordering canonical. It does NOT reduce the test to "compare two log values": the log of the unknown generator is not computable from I without finding the generator; worst case still requires visiting Theta~(R) = 2^{Theta(s)} cycle elements (or sqrt(R) with giant steps). "Reduce by the single unit" IS the right elementary step for NORMALIZING a found generator (k = round(loghat|gamma|_1 / Rhat), gamma' = gamma epsilon^{-k}, done in compact rep by appending epsilon^{-k}) and for verifying a CANDIDATE generator (check (gamma) = I by HNF equality — exact, no precision issue). Precision/height bookkeeping for full rigor with exact arithmetic: (1) ideals always exact (HNF, entries < sqrt(D) after reduction, BW88 Prop 2.11); equality tests exact. (2) real data: certified approximations of sigma_i(omega_j) to 2^{-B}, B = poly(s), from the minimal polynomial (root separation >= 2^{-poly(s)} since disc != 0); every comparison in the Voronoi step compares real algebraic numbers of poly(s) height — decidable exactly because in a cubic field |sigma_2(x)|^2 = |N(x)|/|sigma_1(x)| with N(x) in Z, so all comparisons reduce to comparisons of degree-<=3 real algebraic numbers with poly(s) heights, separated by Mahler-type bounds 2^{-poly(s)} unless equal, and equality is decidable algebraically; (3) accumulated log positions lambda_k are only used against inequalities with constant slack (c1, log sqrt(D), c4, c5), never for equality, so maintaining them to +-2^{-s} (error growth linear in number of steps p = 2^{O(s)} => working precision O(s + log p) = O(s) bits) suffices; (4) minima/relative generators have poly(s)-size coordinates individually (|N(eta)| <= sqrt(D) N(a), steps <= log sqrt(D)); only their PRODUCTS blow up, and those stay as formal compact products.
gaps:
What a self-contained "norm equation solvable in deterministic 2^{O(s)} given certified bnf, fixed degree, rank 1" lemma still needs proved (nothing here is deep, but none of it is verbatim-citable):

1. TRACKED-GENERATOR REDUCTION/JUMP LEMMA: BW88 Thm 4.7/Prop 4.5 decide principality only; BW87 recovers the generator as mu*alpha_k with alpha_k given only by cycle position. Need: "given the cycle position lambda_k (or the walk transcript), a compact representation gamma_0 prod gamma_i^{2^i} of alpha_k with O(s) factors of poly(s) bits is computable deterministically in poly(s) time" — proved by augmenting Schoof Alg 10.8 doubling with generator tracking + height bounds on each relative element (each intermediate ideal reduced, N(I_i^{-1}) <= dF => relative shortest vectors have poly(s) height). Thiel's thesis has the general statement but is not accessible online and its exact complexity claims could not be quoted; must either obtain the thesis / J. Complexity 11 (1995) 310-329, or (recommended) prove the O(s)-factor doubling lemma directly — it is 1 page at rank 1.
2. VERIFY BW CONSTANTS FOR n=3: Prop 2.7(ii) (j,c1)=(7, log 4) read from a degraded scan; confirm against Williams, Pacific J. Math. 124 (1986) 483-496, and confirm Williams' spacing theorem covers ALL complex cubic orders (title says "certain cubic lattices"). Only the EXISTENCE of constants (j, c1) matters for 2^{O(s)}; c1 >= any explicit 2^{-O(1)} suffices.
3. PER-STEP EXACTNESS: BW/Williams per-step O(D^eps) analyses are stated for specific classical representations; a clean lemma "one Voronoi/neighbor step in a complex cubic order is deterministic poly(s) with certified interval arithmetic + algebraic tie-breaking" must be written (the |sigma_2(x)|^2 = |N(x)|/|sigma_1(x)| trick makes ties algebraically decidable; separation via Mahler bounds). Schoof's Alg 10.3 gives the modern LLL version but Schoof's paper is expository and does not carry formal precision proofs.
4. CERTIFIED-BNF INTERFACE: state exactly what is trusted: epsilon is a unit (verifiable exactly: epsilon in O_K, N(epsilon)=+-1, via CRT/modular evaluation of the compact product) AND fundamental (needed ONLY for the one-representative-per-orbit guarantee, not for correctness of the principality decision — a non-fundamental epsilon inflates running time and would break dedup; alternatively fundamentality is re-derivable unconditionally in 2^{O(s)} by the cycle walk itself, BW88 Alg 2.13 + Prop 2.14, making the lemma independent of the unit part of the bnf at rank 1); the class-group part of the bnf is NOT needed at all in the rank-1 route (the cycle walk decides principality directly) — worth stating, since it removes any certification burden on Cl_K.
5. ORBIT/SIGN LEMMA (elementary, must be written): odd degree => solutions of N(gamma)=m up to O_K^* biject with principal ideals of norm |m|; representative extraction and the U^+ coset argument; note this FAILS for even degree (a principal ideal of norm |m| may have no generator of norm m — sign obstruction), so the lemma must be stated for odd n or add a norm-sign check per ideal.
6. IDEAL ENUMERATION LEMMA (elementary): #{ideals of norm |m|} <= d(|m|)^n (or d_n(|m|), citable to the proof line of LEN Thm 6.5 but not to a numbered statement); deterministic listing of primes above p in 2^{O(s)} (trial-division polynomial factorization mod p as in SCH Sec. 11, or subspace brute force; if p divides [O_K:Z[theta]] the polynomial route needs the Buchmann-Lenstra maximal-order factorization [Cohen GTM 138, Alg 6.2.9] — or avoid entirely via the O_K/pO_K maximal-ideal brute force, which needs no citation).
7. If the sharper |Delta|^{1/4+o(1)} decision bound is wanted: import BW88's c4, c5 giant-step constants and their Prop 2.6/2.10 overlap arguments, and reconcile the giant-step baby-stock bound sqrt(R)+c4+c5+log sqrt(D) with a CERTIFIED R (a certified overestimate of R by a bounded factor is harmless; an underestimate is not — needs one paragraph).
8. NOT retrieved despite search: Buchmann's Banff proceedings text (de Gruyter 1990, pp. 37-53) — its Theorems 2 and 4 are known only through Lenstra's citation + misprint corrections (Thm 2: R D^eps; Thm 4: R^{1/2} D^eps, fixed degree, deterministic); and Thiel's thesis / J. Complexity paper full text (paywalled). Neither blocks the lemma (BW88/BW87/SCH/LEN suffice), but exact quotations from these two would strengthen the (b) audit trail.
9. Rank-2 (totally real cubic) if needed: BW88 does NOT apply; the Schoof Sec.-11 assembly (scan identity component, membership, tracked generators) must be written out — all tools are in SCH Secs. 7, 10, 11 with stated complexities, but the PIP glue and generator tracking are folklore (about 2 pages, dimension-constant CVP/mesh in a 2-torus).

citations.ref: [SCH] R. Schoof, Computing Arakelov class groups, in: Algorithmic Number Theory, MSRI Publ. 44 (2008), 447-495. Secs. 7, 10, 11 (Lemma 11.1, Alg 11.2, deterministic sqrt(|Delta|)-algorithm, Props 7.1-7.2, Thm 7.7, Cors 7.8-7.10, Prop 7.11, Algs 10.3-10.8)

[LEN] H. W. Lenstra, Jr., Algorithms in algebraic number theory, Bull. AMS 26 (1992) 211-244. Thms 5.4, 5.5, 6.5; Problems 5.1, 5.6; Sec. 5 PIP-difficulty remark; proof of Thm 6.5 for the ideal-count bound

[BW88] J. Buchmann, H. C. Williams, On the infrastructure of the principal ideal class of an algebraic number field of unit rank one, Math. Comp. 50 (1988) 569-579. Props 2.4-2.14, Cor 2.8, Thm 3.3, Alg 4.4, Prop 4.5, Alg 4.6, Thm 4.7
[BW87] J. Buchmann, H. C. Williams, On principal ideal testing in algebraic number fields, J. Symbolic Comput. 4 (1987) 11-19. Algorithm 1.1 (generator recovery a = (mu alpha_k))

[BF14] J.-F. Biasse, C. Fieker, Subexponential class group and unit group computation in large degree number fields, LMS J. Comput. Math. 17A (2014) 385-403. Sec. 5 (compact representations; 'An exponential algorithm was presented in [35]' = Thiel's thesis), Alg 7, Prop 5.1, Thms 6.1-6.2

[THI94] C. Thiel, Under the assumption of the Generalized Riemann Hypothesis verifying the class number belongs to NP cap co-NP, ANTS-I, LNCS 877 (1994) 234-247 (abstract: fundamental units in (2+log_2 Delta)^{O(1)} bits via compact representations)

[THI95] C. Thiel, Short proofs using compact representations of algebraic integers, J. Complexity 11 (1995) 310-329; and PhD thesis, Universitaet des Saarlandes, 1995 (Cor 3.7 cited via Fontein-Jacobson; compact-representation computation, exponential time, cited via BF14). Full text not freely retrievable

[FJ10] F. Fontein, M. J. Jacobson Jr., Rigorous computation of fundamental units in algebraic number fields, arXiv:1001.4187 (2010). Quotes [Thi95, Cor 3.7]; Buchmann neighbor-graph connectivity [Buc87a]; minima storage bounds

[BAN] J. Buchmann, Complexity of algorithms in algebraic number theory, Proc. First Conf. Canadian Number Theory Assoc. (Banff, 1988), de Gruyter, 1990, 37-53. NOT retrieved online; Theorems 2 (R D^eps) and 4 (R^{1/2} D^eps) known via Lenstra's misprint-corrected citation

[EH13] K. Eisentraeger, S. Hallgren, Computing the unit group, class group, and compact representations in algebraic function fields, ANTS-X, Open Book Series 1 (2013) 17-34 (attribution of number-field compact-rep results to Thiel; PIP in NP via compact representations)

[QS24] Upper bounding the quantum space complexity for computing class group and principal ideal problem, arXiv:2405.12508 (quotes Biasse-Song SODA 2016 Thm 1.1: quantum poly-time S-units in compact representation) - context only

[vG25] D. M. H. van Gent (lectures by H. W. Lenstra), Polynomial-time algorithms in algebraic number theory, arXiv:2502.19036 - polytime ideal-arithmetic toolkit (HNF, two-element representations, coprime bases); deliberately avoids prime-ideal factorization
Williams, The spacing of the minima in certain cubic lattices, Pacific J. Math. 124 (1986) 483-496 (source of BW88 Prop 2.7(ii) n=3 constants - verify (j,c1)=(7,log 4))

Buchmann-Thiel-Williams, Short representation of quadratic integers, in Computational Algebra and Number Theory, Math. Appl. 325, Springer (1995) 159-185 (quadratic prototype of compact representations)
